frameworks.name: neoclassical utility maximization
frameworks.one_line: Optimal-agent choice theory assuming no internal computational resource constraints.

frameworks.name: bounded rationality / resource-constrained valuation
frameworks.one_line: Brain implements modular valuation under severe computational resource limits.

frameworks.name: common-currency decision variable
frameworks.one_line: Neural data validate a single decision variable computed per option during choice.

core_question: What proximate neurobiological mechanisms produce individual decision-making behavior, and how do they explain failures of neoclassical maximization?
key_assumptions: Decision makers compute and represent a single-decision variable per option
Human brain faces severe computational resource constraints
Valuation and choice rely on modular processing
manipulations: Synthesizes evidence across neurobiology, psychology, economics, and evolutionary approaches (review chapter — no experimental manipulation)
key_findings: Humans empirically fail to achieve maximization across health, happiness, and wealth domains
Neurobiology validates the single-decision-variable assumption from economics/psychology
Neural data reveal brain reliance on modular valuation/choice approaches under resource limits
interpretations: Computational resource constraints explain systematic failures of neoclassical optimization
Modular valuation mechanisms open new theoretical avenues beyond multi-parameter psychological models
